title: Eremophila kraenzlinii
intro: Eremophila kraenzlinii, also known as the Western Australian emu bush, is a flowering plant species native to Western Australia, belonging to the family Scrophulariaceae. This plant has been traditionally used in Western Australian Aboriginal medicine, and its extracts have been found to possess various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antioxidant properties, which may aid in the treatment of wounds, skin conditions, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Eremophila kraenzlinii include iridoid glycosides, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Eremophila kraenzlinii is commonly used in herbal preparations, such as teas, tinctures, and topical creams, to leverage its medicinal benefits and support traditional healing practices.
taxonomy: Eremophila kraenzlinii is classified within the family Scrophulariaceae, which is a family of flowering plants commonly known as the figwort family. This family is further divided into several genera, with Eremophila being a part of the Myoporum clade. Eremophila kraenzlinii belongs to the genus Eremophila, which comprises over 200 species of shrubs native to Australia. The genus Eremophila is a member of the tribe Myoporeae, which is a group of flowering plants that are characterized by their five-merous flowers. Eremophila kraenzlinii is specifically classified as Eremophila kraenzlinii within the plant kingdom, with its full taxonomic classification being Eremophila kraenzlinii (Diels) E.Pritz.

The leaves of the plant are said to be used to treat various ailments, including wounds and skin irritations.
